title: The Crater Petersburg Never Forgot
summary: Interviewed by David Cline at Wesley Theological Seminary on December 14, 2015, Maynard E. Moore, a Washington resident of 35 years, says he was born in Petersburg, Virginia in 1938, and recounts the Civil War battle there in which Union troops tunneled under the Confederate works and blew them up by an accidental trigger, the site now preserved as the Crater and remembered as a prominent event in the town's history.

title: Washington Street and the Methodist Schism
summary: Moore describes Petersburg as a commercial railhead for tobacco boosted by the Army quartermaster depot at Fort Lee in World War II, his Methodist family at Washington Street United Methodist Church among the town's ten Methodist churches, and a placard marking his home church as the 1846 site where the Methodist Episcopal Church, South broke from the northern church over slavery, the church where he felt the call to ministry and was elected to statewide office in the Methodist Student Movement.

title: No More Welcome in the House
summary: Moore recalls that racial issues went unaddressed by design in his childhood, that he lived on a main street while Black residents lived on the less cared-for cross streets, and that he played with Black children and invited them in to watch Howdy Doody until, at the time for junior high school, his mother explained they would no longer be welcome in the house.
